commands.repair_reason: - [P1] A focused repair can carry requester source through the MCP path and add targeted cross-source regression proof; maintainer compatibility approval is still needed afterward.; Needs attention: The diff improves sender-context trust overall, but the MCP loopback path still has a concrete cross-source requester attribution gap.; Review finding: [P1] Carry requester source through MCP loopback — src/agents/cli-ru... (sha=4962aa471672b052a116227f8f3c1e24ac0c792e)
commands.review_summary:
The branch carries requester identity through Discord/WhatsApp message actions, Gateway `message.action`, CLI/MCP loopback, and related tests so privileged Discord actions require trusted sender context.

PR surface: Source +495, Tests +787. Total +1282 across 40 files.

Reproducibility: yes. source inspection gives a high-confidence path: a sender-scoped MCP run with a routeable `messageChannel` and different `messageProvider` exposes `senderId` to message tools without preserving requester source. I did not execute that failing path in this read-only review.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] A focused repair can carry requester source through the MCP path and add targeted cross-source regression proof; maintainer compatibility approval is still needed afterward.

target.title: fix(discord): require sender context for moderation actions
target.body:
## Summary

- Require trusted Discord requester context before privileged Discord moderation and guild-admin message actions can use requester identity.
- Carry requester identity through CLI, MCP loopback, and gateway message-action paths without relying on client-controlled sender headers.
- Keep deliverable channel routing on the routeable provider while carrying non-deliverable requester source providers separately.
- Bind sender-scoped MCP loopback tokens to the current request context so requester identity cannot be replayed into another session/channel envelope.
- Strip `toolContext.requesterSourceProvider` from non-admin gateway `message.action` callers so requester source trust stays coupled to server-authorized sender identity.
- Honor `requesterSourceProvider` for WhatsApp reaction requester trust so cross-source reactions cannot preserve the wrong sender identity.

## Compatibility / scope notes

- This intentionally fails closed for tool-driven Discord privileged actions from non-Discord requester contexts. Existing cross-channel automation that asks a Discord guild-admin/moderation action to run without a trusted Discord requester or trusted local owner context will now receive an error instead of executing without trusted sender attribution.
- Removed unrelated heartbeat and Gateway WS cosmetic/test hunks from the PR. The `src/agents/tools/pdf-tool.model-config.test.ts` metadata fixture remains because the broad proof suite fails without an explicit manifest metadata snapshot for those PDF model-config assertions.

## Verification

- `git diff --name-only origin/main..HEAD -- '*.ts' '*.mjs' | xargs -r ./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo`
- `git diff --check origin/main..HEAD`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/whatsapp/src/channel-actions.test.ts extensions/discord/src/channel-actions.test.ts src/channels/plugins/message-actions.security.test.ts src/infra/outbound/message-action-runner.plugin-dispatch.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/send.test.ts src/gateway/mcp-http.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec tsx -` with a redacted runtime proof script importing `dispatchChannelMessageAction`, `sendHandlers["message.action"]`, and MCP loopback validation helpers.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Discord privileged moderation and guild-admin message actions now require a trusted Discord requester or trusted local owner context; Gateway strips non-admin spoofed requester sender/source context; MCP loopback sender tokens are bound to the signed channel/session/message envelope; WhatsApp reactions only preserve requester identity for same-source WhatsApp requester context.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on Linux/UTC, Node `v22.22.2`, pnpm `11.2.2`, branch `discord-action-sender-guard`, exact PR head `4962aa471672b052a116227f8f3c1e24ac0c792e`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
git diff --name-only origin/main..HEAD -- '*.ts' '*.mjs' | xargs -r ./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo
git diff --check origin/main..HEAD
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/whatsapp/src/channel-actions.test.ts extensions/discord/src/channel-actions.test.ts src/channels/plugins/message-actions.security.test.ts src/infra/outbound/message-action-runner.plugin-dispatch.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/send.test.ts src/gateway/mcp-http.test.ts
pnpm exec tsx - <<'EOF'
# Redacted runtime proof script executed against OpenClaw runtime modules:
# - dispatchChannelMessageAction with Discord trusted-requester helper
# - sendHandlers["message.action"] with non-admin/admin Gateway contexts
# - validateMcpLoopbackRequest with matching and mismatched scoped MCP context
EOF
```

Evidence after fix: Copied terminal output from the local run on the exact PR head:
```text
$ node --version
v22.22.2
$ pnpm --version
11.2.2
$ git rev-parse HEAD
4962aa471672b052a116227f8f3c1e24ac0c792e

$ git diff --name-only origin/main..HEAD -- '*.ts' '*.mjs' | xargs -r ./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1
Checking formatting...
All matched files use the correct format.
Finished in 146ms on 40 files using 1 threads.

$ node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo
(exit 0; no diagnostics)

$ git diff --check origin/main..HEAD
(exit 0; no diagnostics)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/whatsapp/src/channel-actions.test.ts extensions/discord/src/channel-actions.test.ts src/channels/plugins/message-actions.security.test.ts src/infra/outbound/message-action-runner.plugin-dispatch.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/send.test.ts src/gateway/mcp-http.test.ts
[test] queued behind the local heavy-check lock held by tsgo...
Test Files 6 passed (6); Tests 232 passed (232) [gateway shard]
Test Files 1 passed (1); Tests 22 passed (22) [infra shard]
Test Files 1 passed (1); Tests 28 passed (28) [discord extension shard]
Test Files 1 passed (1); Tests 12 passed (12) [whatsapp extension shard]
Test Files 1 passed (1); Tests 3 passed (3) [channels shard]
[test] passed 5 Vitest shards in 54.65s

$ pnpm exec tsx -
[proof] OpenClaw runtime sender-context proof for PR #90481
[proof] git HEAD 4962aa471672b052a116227f8f3c1e24ac0c792e
[proof] Discord source-helper dispatch: kick without requesterSenderId => BLOCKED before action handler
[proof] Gateway message.action non-admin spoof: requesterSenderId=<none> requesterSourceProvider=<none> senderIsOwner=false
[proof] Gateway message.action admin scoped: requesterSenderId=<redacted> requesterSourceProvider=discord senderIsOwner=true
[proof] MCP loopback scoped token match: senderId=<redacted> messageProvider=discord senderIsOwner=false
[proof] MCP loopback scoped token mismatch: status=401 body={"error":"unauthorized"}
[proof] PASS: 2 Gateway action(s), Discord dispatch block, MCP scoped-context match/mismatch
```

Observed result after fix: Discord privileged action dispatch fails closed before the action handler when requester sender identity is missing. Gateway `message.action` strips spoofed non-admin `requesterSenderId`, `senderIsOwner`, and `toolContext.requesterSourceProvider`, while admin-scoped calls preserve the trusted context. MCP loopback accepts a sender-scoped token only when request headers match the signed Discord source context and returns 401 for a mismatched provider. WhatsApp reaction tests now cover the cross-source case where `currentChannelProvider` is WhatsApp but `requesterSourceProvider` is not, and the inverse trusted same-source requester case.

What was not tested: No live Discord guild moderation command was executed against a real Discord bot/guild; the runtime proof intentionally stops at the pre-network trust boundary and uses redacted local Gateway/MCP runtime calls.
